content.text: How do I rotate a given 3D point (x, y, z) from its original Cartesian reference frame into a new orientation?

This function achieves coordinate rotation by applying the stored rotation matrix to the input vector using a dot product calculation.

```python
def transformCartesianCoordinates(self, x, y, z):
        """
        Rotates Cartesian coordinates from one reference system to another using the rotation matrix with
        which the class was initialized. The inputs  can be scalars or 1-dimensional numpy arrays.

        Parameters
        ----------

        x - Value of X-coordinate in original reference system
        y - Value of Y-coordinate in original reference system
        z - Value of Z-coordinate in original reference system

        Returns
        -------

        xrot - Value of X-coordinate after rotation
        yrot - Value of Y-coordinate after rotation
        zrot - Value of Z-coordinate after rotation
        """
        xrot, yrot, zrot = dot(self.rotationMatrix,[x,y,z])
        return xrot, yrot, zrot
```

The method effectively applies the linear transformation defined by `self.rotationMatrix` to your spatial coordinates, resulting in the rotated values.
Since it handles scalar and array inputs, what happens if I pass in non-numeric data like strings?
If you provide non-numeric data, the dot function will raise a TypeError because matrix multiplication requires numerical types. You must ensure all inputs are comparable to the rotation matrix.
That makes sense; input validation is key.
